explanation.simple: A point is an exact location in space with no size. A line is an infinite collection of points extending in both directions with no width. A plane is a flat surface that extends infinitely in all directions. These are the building blocks of all geometry!
explanation.advanced: In Euclidean geometry (as axiomatized by Hilbert), a point is an undefined primitive with no dimension (dimension 0). A line is a 1-dimensional locus defined by any two distinct points, extending infinitely. A plane is a 2-dimensional flat surface determined by three non-collinear points. Euclid's five postulates govern these primitives: (1) a straight line can be drawn between any two points, (2) a line segment can be extended indefinitely, (3) a circle can be drawn with any center and radius, (4) all right angles are equal, and (5) the parallel postulate.

m:n.description: Ratio in which P divides the segment

θ.description: Angle line makes with positive x-axis

compass.description: Draw a circle with a given center and radius
compass.usage: Click a center point, drag to set radius

straightedge.description: Draw a straight line through two points
straightedge.usage: Click two points to draw a line segment

real_world_examples.title: GPS Navigation
real_world_examples.description: GPS uses the distance formula in 3D (with Earth's curvature corrections) to calculate distances between your location and your destination.

real_world_examples.title: Architecture
real_world_examples.description: Architects use points, lines, and planes to design buildings. Blueprints are 2D representations of 3D structures using geometric primitives.

mcq.question: The distance between points (1, 2) and (4, 6) is:
Two lines with slopes m₁ and m₂ are perpendicular if:
mcq.explanation: Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals: m₁ × m₂ = -1.

short_answer.question: Find the equation of the line passing through (2, 3) with slope 4.
short_answer.answer: Using point-slope form: y - 3 = 4(x - 2) → y = 4x - 8 + 3 → y = 4x - 5
short_answer.hint: Use y - y₁ = m(x - x₁) with the given point and slope.

explanation.simple: An angle is formed when two rays share a common endpoint (vertex). When a line crosses two parallel lines, it creates special angle pairs: alternate angles are equal, corresponding angles are equal, and co-interior angles add up to 180°. These rules help us solve many geometry problems!
explanation.advanced: An angle is the figure formed by two rays (sides) sharing a common endpoint (vertex). Angles are measured in degrees (0°–360°) or radians (0–2π). Euclid's postulate 4 states all right angles are congruent. When a transversal intersects parallel lines, the following angle relationships follow from Euclid's 5th postulate (parallel postulate): corresponding angles are congruent (F-pattern), alternate interior angles are congruent (Z-pattern), alternate exterior angles are congruent, and co-interior (same-side interior) angles are supplementary.

α, β.description: Adjacent angles on a straight line (supplementary)

highlight_mode.description: Click an angle pair type to highlight the matching angles in the diagram

real_world_examples.title: Railroad Tracks
real_world_examples.description: Railroad tracks are parallel lines. The railway ties act as transversals, creating equal corresponding angles.

real_world_examples.title: Scissor Lifts
real_world_examples.description: Scissor lift mechanisms use the properties of parallel lines and equal alternate angles to raise platforms evenly.

mcq.question: If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal making a 65° angle with one line, the alternate interior angle is:
mcq.explanation: Alternate interior angles are equal when lines are parallel. So the angle is 65°.

mcq.question: Co-interior angles on the same side of a transversal sum to:
mcq.explanation: Co-interior (same-side interior) angles are supplementary: they add up to 180°.

mcq.question: Vertically opposite angles are always:
mcq.explanation: When two lines intersect, the vertically opposite angles are always equal.

short_answer.question: A transversal crosses two parallel lines. One angle is 72°. Find all eight angles formed at both intersection points.
short_answer.answer: At each intersection: 72°, 108°, 72°, 108° (vertically opposite pairs). Both intersections have the same four angles due to parallel line properties.
short_answer.hint: Use: supplementary angles sum to 180°, vertically opposite angles are equal, corresponding angles are equal.

explanation.simple: A triangle is a polygon with three sides, three vertices, and three angles. The angles of any triangle always add up to 180°. Triangles can be classified by sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene) or by angles (acute, right, obtuse). The Pythagoras theorem is special for right triangles: a² + b² = c².
explanation.advanced: A triangle is the simplest polygon, defined by three non-collinear points. Key properties include: angle sum = 180° (provable via the parallel postulate), triangle inequality (sum of any two sides > third side), and area formulas (½bh, Heron's formula, cross product). Congruence criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS) establish when two triangles are identical in shape and size. Similarity criteria (AA, SAS, SSS) establish proportional correspondence. Notable theorems include: Pythagoras, angle bisector theorem, basic proportionality theorem (Thales), and the centroid divides medians in 2:1 ratio.

formulas.name: Exterior Angle Theorem
formulas.latex: \text{exterior angle} = \text{sum of two non-adjacent interior angles}

theorem_proofs.statement: In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides.
proof_steps: Consider a right triangle ABC with right angle at C.
Draw altitude CD from C to hypotenuse AB, where D is the foot.
Triangle ACD is similar to triangle ABC (AA similarity: ∠A is common, ∠ACD = ∠ABC = 90° - ∠A).
So AC/AB = AD/AC → AC² = AB × AD ... (i)
Similarly, triangle BCD is similar to triangle BAC.
So BC/BA = BD/BC → BC² = AB × BD ... (ii)
Adding (i) and (ii): AC² + BC² = AB × AD + AB × BD = AB(AD + BD) = AB × AB = AB²
Therefore: a² + b² = c². ∎
theorem_proofs.visualization_hint: Show triangle ABC with altitude CD. Highlight the three similar triangles with different colors.

theorem_proofs.statement: The sum of interior angles of a triangle is 180°.
proof_steps: Let triangle ABC have angles ∠A, ∠B, ∠C.
Draw a line PQ through vertex A, parallel to side BC.
∠PAB = ∠ABC (alternate interior angles with transversal AB, since PQ ∥ BC).
∠QAC = ∠ACB (alternate interior angles with transversal AC, since PQ ∥ BC).
Now, ∠PAB + ∠BAC + ∠QAC = 180° (angles on a straight line PQ).
Substituting: ∠ABC + ∠BAC + ∠ACB = 180°.
Therefore: ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180°. ∎
theorem_proofs.visualization_hint: Show triangle with line through vertex A parallel to BC. Color-code alternate angle pairs.

theorem_proofs.statement: If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, it divides the other two sides proportionally.
proof_steps: In triangle ABC, let DE ∥ BC, where D is on AB and E is on AC.
We need to prove: AD/DB = AE/EC.
Join BE and CD.
Area of △ADE = ½ × AD × h₁ (where h₁ is perpendicular from E to AD).
Area of △BDE = ½ × DB × h₁ (same height h₁).
So ar(△ADE)/ar(△BDE) = AD/DB ... (i)
Similarly, ar(△ADE)/ar(△CDE) = AE/EC ... (ii)
Since DE ∥ BC, triangles BDE and CDE have the same base DE and lie between the same parallels.
Therefore ar(△BDE) = ar(△CDE) ... (iii)
From (i), (ii), and (iii): AD/DB = AE/EC. ∎

construction.description: Compass + straightedge construction of equilateral triangle
steps: Set compass to length AB. Place at A, draw arc.
Place compass at B, draw arc.
Mark intersection C of the two arcs.

real_world_examples.title: Bridge Trusses
real_world_examples.description: Triangular shapes are the strongest structural form because they distribute weight evenly. Bridge trusses, roof supports, and cranes all use triangles.

real_world_examples.title: Surveying & Navigation
real_world_examples.description: Triangulation uses the properties of triangles to determine distances and positions of distant objects, used in GPS and land surveying.

mcq.question: If two angles of a triangle are 50° and 70°, the third angle is:
In a right triangle with legs 3 and 4, the hypotenuse is:
How many congruence criteria exist for triangles?
mcq.explanation: Five: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and RHS (right angle-hypotenuse-side).

mcq.question: The centroid divides each median in the ratio:
mcq.explanation: The centroid divides each median in the ratio 2:1 from vertex to midpoint of opposite side.

short_answer.question: Prove that an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
short_answer.answer: Let ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180° (angle sum). The exterior angle at C = 180° - ∠C (linear pair). So exterior angle = 180° - ∠C = ∠A + ∠B.
short_answer.hint: Use the angle sum property and the fact that an angle and its exterior angle form a linear pair (180°).

explanation.simple: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are at the same distance (radius) from a fixed point (center). Key parts include: radius, diameter, chord, arc, sector, segment, and tangent. Circles have many beautiful properties that have fascinated mathematicians for thousands of years!
explanation.advanced: A circle is the locus of points equidistant (radius r) from a center (h, k), with equation (x-h)² + (y-k)² = r². Key theorems include: angle at center = 2 × angle at circumference (same arc), angles in a semicircle = 90°, cyclic quadrilateral opposite angles sum to 180°, tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of contact, and the alternate segment theorem. The area = πr² (provable by integration or Archimedes' exhaustion method) and circumference = 2πr.

θ.description: Central angle subtended by the arc

formulas.name: Length of tangent from external point

d.description: Distance from external point to center

theorem_proofs.statement: The angle subtended by an arc at the center is double the angle subtended at any point on the remaining arc.
proof_steps: Let O be the center, and arc PQ subtend ∠POQ at center and ∠PAQ at point A on the circle.
Join AO and extend to point R beyond O.
In triangle OAP: OA = OP (radii), so ∠OAP = ∠OPA (isosceles triangle).
Exterior angle ∠POR = ∠OAP + ∠OPA = 2∠OAP ... (i)
Similarly, in triangle OAQ: ∠QOR = 2∠OAQ ... (ii)
Adding (i) and (ii): ∠POQ = ∠POR + ∠QOR = 2(∠OAP + ∠OAQ) = 2∠PAQ.
Therefore: ∠POQ = 2 × ∠PAQ. ∎

theorem_proofs.statement: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is always 90°.
proof_steps: Let AB be a diameter of circle with center O.
Let C be any point on the semicircle (not on AB).
∠AOB = 180° (straight line / diameter).
By the angle at the center theorem: ∠ACB = ½ × ∠AOB = ½ × 180° = 90°.
Therefore, angle in a semicircle is always a right angle. ∎

construction.description: Compass construction of tangent from external point
steps: Mark external point P and center O.
Draw circle with center M and radius MO = MP.
Mark intersection points T₁, T₂ with original circle.
Lines PT₁ and PT₂ are tangents. OT₁ ⊥ PT₁.

real_world_examples.title: Wheels & Gears
real_world_examples.description: Wheels work because every point on the rim is equidistant from the axle (center). Gear ratios depend on the circumference relationship between meshing circles.

real_world_examples.title: Satellite Orbits
real_world_examples.description: Low Earth orbit satellites follow approximately circular paths. The orbital speed depends on the radius: v = √(GM/r).

mcq.question: The area of a circle with diameter 10 cm is:
A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the:
options: Radius at the point of contact
mcq.explanation: A fundamental property: the tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point.

mcq.question: The angle inscribed in a semicircle is:
mcq.explanation: By the angle in a semicircle theorem, any angle inscribed in a semicircle is always 90°.

mcq.question: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral sum to:
mcq.explanation: In a cyclic quadrilateral (inscribed in a circle), opposite angles are supplementary: ∠A + ∠C = 180°, ∠B + ∠D = 180°.

short_answer.question: A chord is 8 cm long and is 3 cm from the center. Find the radius.
short_answer.answer: Half-chord = 4 cm. Using Pythagoras: r² = 4² + 3² = 16 + 9 = 25 → r = 5 cm.
short_answer.hint: The perpendicular from the center bisects the chord. Use the right triangle formed by half-chord, distance, and radius.
